1: In that day you will say: I praise you, YHWH; though you were angry with me, your anger has turned and you have comforted me. The doxology opens with the same formula as Isaiah 2:11, 17, 20 — in that day — marking the transition from judgment (chapters 1-11) to restored worship. The anger-turned-to-comfort is the exact theological movement of the Servant's work: YHWH's wrath against sin is absorbed (Isaiah 53:5-6) so that comfort can reach the people (Isaiah 40:1). Paul quotes the spirit of this verse when he writes that we were under wrath but have been reconciled through the Son (Romans 5:10-11). The one who praises YHWH for anger-turned-to-comfort is the remnant saved through judgment — the community of the new creation.
2: God is my salvation! I will trust and will not be afraid; for YHWH, YHWH himself, is my strength and my song; he has become my salvation. This verse directly quotes the Song of Moses after the Red Sea crossing (Exodus 15:2) and Psalms 118:14 — all three contexts celebrate YHWH's salvific victory. The Isaianic reuse signals: the new exodus (chapters 40-55) has now arrived; its doxology repeats the first exodus doxology. The doubling of the divine name (YHWH YHWH) for emphasis is unique. In the NT, Jesus is the culmination of this salvation-pattern: the one in whom YHWH himself becomes salvation (Matthew 1:21, his name is Jesus — YHWH saves). Zechariah's Benedictus (Luke 1:68-79) uses the same exodus-salvation vocabulary for the coming of the Messiah.
3: With joy you will draw water from the springs of salvation. The water-from-springs image echoes the wilderness water-from-rock (Exodus 17:6, Numbers 20:8-11) and anticipates the rivers-of-living-water of Isaiah 41:17-18, Isaiah 44:3. Jesus applies this image directly to himself at the Feast of Tabernacles: if anyone thirsts, let him come to me and drink; out of his heart will flow rivers of living water (John 7:37-38, citing Isaiah 55:1 and this verse). The springs of salvation are the inexhaustible gift of the Spirit that the Messiah provides — the water that becomes a spring welling up to eternal life (John 4:14).
4: Give thanks to YHWH; call on his name; make his deeds known among the nations; proclaim that his name is exalted. The praise breaks outward from the individual (v 1) to the community (v 3) to the nations (v 4) — the centrifugal movement of covenant blessing. Calling on the name of YHWH is the OT definition of true worship (Genesis 4:26, Isaiah 12:8); in the NT it becomes the specific act of confessing the Lordship of Jesus (Romans 10:9-13, Joel 2:32 / Acts 2:21). Making YHWH's deeds known among the nations is the Servant's commission (Isaiah 42:1, Isaiah 49:6) and the church's mission (Matthew 28:19-20).
5: Sing praises to YHWH, for he has done glorious things; make this known throughout all the earth! The glorious things (gaavah) that YHWH has done are the specific redemptive acts: exodus, Sinai, return from exile, and ultimately the Servant's atonement and resurrection. The universal proclamation — make this known throughout all the earth — is the scope of the Great Commission. The NT hymnody of Revelation 15:3-4 (great and marvelous are your deeds, Lord God Almighty) echoes this verse as the song of the redeemed from every nation standing before the throne.
6: Cry aloud and shout for joy, you who dwell in Zion, for great is the Holy One of Israel in your midst! The doxology ends with the Isaianic title Holy One of Israel — the name introduced in 1:4 and central to the whole book's theology. YHWH's holiness is not a distant quality but in your midst — the Shekinah presence dwelling in the community (Isaiah 4:5-6). In the NT, the indwelling of the Holy Spirit makes every believer and the assembled church the dwelling of the Holy One: you are the temple of the Holy Spirit (1 Corinthians 6:19, Isaiah 3:16). The shout of joy over the Holy One in the midst is Pentecostal worship — the reality John 7:39 and Acts 2 describe as the Spirit poured out on the community.
